skill.name: etetoolkit
skill.description: ETE (Environment for Tree Exploration) toolkit for phylogenetic and hierarchical tree analysis; use it when you need to parse/manipulate Newick/NHX trees, detect duplication/speciation events, integrate NCBI taxonomy, and render publication-quality figures.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for phylogenetic tree analysis, manipulation, and visualization using the ETE Python toolkit.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: After AI evaluation, all 398 static analysis findings are dismissed as false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are ETE Newick format codes and Python code examples, not cryptography. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are Markdown code fence blocks showing Python examples. The 'System reconnaissance' detections are NCBI taxonomy API queries for legitimate phylogenetic research. The only confirmed finding is documentation about NCBITaxa downloading a taxonomy database to ~/.etetoolkit/taxa.sqlite, which is standard ETE library behavior and disclosed to users. No malicious intent, obfuscation, or credential access patterns detected. Risk level is low.

low_findings.title: NCBITaxa database download to home directory documented
low_findings.description: Documentation at references/api_reference.md:72 states that NCBITaxa() will download a ~300MB taxonomy database to ~/.etetoolkit/taxa.sqlite on first use. This is standard ETE library behavior, not malicious. The path is disclosed to users. Confidence: LOW because this is normal library behavior documented transparently.

content.user_title: Analyze and Visualize Phylogenetic Trees with ETE Toolkit
content.value_statement: Phylogenetic tree analysis requires specialized tools for parsing Newick and NHX formats, detecting evolutionary events, and generating publication-ready figures. This skill provides expert guidance for using the ETE Python toolkit to manipulate phylogenetic trees, integrate NCBI taxonomy data, and create professional visualizations.
actual_capabilities: Parse and manipulate phylogenetic trees in Newick, NHX, PhyloXML, and NeXML formats
Detect duplication versus speciation events in gene trees for ortholog analysis
Integrate NCBI taxonomy data including TaxID lookup, lineage retrieval, and rank information
Generate publication-quality tree visualizations in PDF, SVG, and PNG formats with custom styles
Compare tree topologies using Robinson-Foulds distance and partition analysis
Perform clustering analysis with dendrogram and combined heatmap views
limitations: Does not perform phylogenetic inference or tree-building from raw sequence data
NCBI taxonomy database requires internet access for initial download of ~300MB
Visualization rendering depends on local system libraries such as PyQt5
Does not support species tree inference or gene tree reconciliation methods

use_cases.title: Phylogenomics Gene Family Analysis
use_cases.description: A researcher needs to analyze gene family evolution across multiple species. Use this skill to parse gene trees, detect duplication versus speciation events, and extract ortholog groups for downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Publication-Ready Tree Figures
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs to create professional phylogenetic tree figures for a manuscript. Use this skill to style trees with support values, custom node colors, and export to PDF or SVG vector formats.

use_cases.title: Taxonomy-Annotated Tree Building
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician needs to annotate a species tree with taxonomic lineages. Use this skill to integrate NCBI taxonomy data, retrieve taxonomic ranks, and build annotated tree topologies.

prompt_templates.title: Load and Explore a Phylogenetic Tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Load a phylogenetic tree from a Newick file and display its basic statistics including number of leaves, total nodes, tree depth, and branch length distribution.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a Newick tree file and need to understand its structure before further analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Detect Duplication and Speciation Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Load a gene tree and detect duplication versus speciation events. Provide a species naming function to map gene IDs to species names, then identify ortholog and paralog groups.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a gene tree and need to distinguish orthologs from paralogs for phylogenomic analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Tree Visualization for Publication
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a publication-quality tree visualization with custom node colors, support value labels, a scale bar, and export to PDF. Color internal nodes based on support thresholds.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a styled phylogenetic tree figure for inclusion in a journal article.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate NCBI Taxonomy Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NCBITaxa to look up taxonomic information for a list of species. Retrieve TaxIDs, scientific names, lineage paths, and taxonomic ranks. Build a minimal taxonomy tree connecting the species.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a list of species names and need to add taxonomic annotations to your phylogenetic analysis.

output_examples.input: Load a Newick tree and display statistics
output_examples.output:
Number of leaves: 12
Total nodes: 23
Tree depth: 0.8500
Branch length statistics:
  Mean: 0.1234
  Min: 0.0100
  Max: 0.3500

output_examples.input: Render a styled tree to PDF with support-based coloring
output_examples.output: A PDF file showing a rectangular phylogenetic tree with blue leaf nodes, red or green internal nodes based on support value thresholds, branch support labels, and a scale bar at the bottom.

output_examples.input: Detect evolutionary events in a gene tree
output_examples.output:
Duplication events found at node: GeneFamily_001 (D)
Speciation events found at node: SpeciesClade_A (S)
Ortholog groups extracted: 3 groups
Paralog groups extracted: 2 groups

best_practices: Specify the correct Newick format number when loading trees to ensure proper parsing of branch lengths, support values, and internal node names.
Use preserve_branch_length=True when pruning trees to maintain accurate phylogenetic distances for downstream analyses.
Cache NCBI taxonomy results locally and use batch queries to minimize database access during large-scale analyses.
anti_patterns: Using the default format=0 for all tree files without verifying the actual Newick format of input data.
Applying midpoint rooting without first checking if the tree is already rooted or understanding its topology.
Calling NCBITaxa constructor without handling potential network errors on systems without internet access.

faq.question: What file formats does the ETE toolkit support?
faq.answer: ETE supports Newick, NHX, PhyloXML, and NeXML formats for tree input and output. Newick is the most common format for phylogenetic trees.

faq.question: How do I install the ETE toolkit?
faq.answer: Install ETE using pip: pip install ete3. Optional visualization dependencies include PyQt5 and Qt SVG support on Linux systems.

faq.question: What does the Newick format number mean?
faq.answer: The format number controls how ETE interprets node attributes. Format 0 is flexible, format 1 includes internal node names, format 2 includes support values, and format 8 includes all features.

faq.question: How do I detect gene duplication events?
faq.answer: Use PhyloTree with a species naming function. Load a gene tree, provide a function that maps gene IDs to species names, and call get_descendant_evol_events() to detect duplication and speciation events.

faq.question: Does the skill require internet access?
faq.answer: Most tree operations work offline. NCBI taxonomy integration requires internet for the initial database download of approximately 300MB. After download, the database is cached locally.

faq.question: How do I compare two phylogenetic trees?
faq.answer: Use the robinson_foulds() method to compute the Robinson-Foulds distance between two trees. This returns the RF distance, maximum possible RF, and shared leaf information for normalization.
